name: Sanur / Sanur Reef / Pantai Sanur (incl. Hyatt Reef, Tandjung Lefts, Sindhu)
gps_location: https://maps.app.goo.gl/?q=-8.6905,115.2620 (approx. -8.6905, 115.2620, Sanur Reef ~1 km offshore in front of Grand Inna Bali Beach Hotel / Fairmont Sanur Beach, Denpasar) [uncertain]
distance_from_dps_airport_km: approx. 14-16 km (about 25-35 min by car from DPS Ngurah Rai Airport)
distance_from_canggu_uluwatu_min: From Canggu: ~45-60 min by scooter (~20-25 km via Sunset Road/bypass); From Uluwatu: ~45-60 min by scooter (~25-30 km) [uncertain]
wave_type: Right-hand reef break ~1 km offshore (Bali's longest paddle); fast, hollow barrels on main South peak with steep powerful take-off; the wider Sanur reef system actually has 4 named peaks - Sanur (Sanur Reef/Bomby), Hyatt Reef, Tandjung Sari (Sindhu Right + Sindhu Left), and Tandjung Lefts
skill_level: Intermediate to Advanced (Sanur Reef main peak is advanced/pro on bigger days; Hyatt Reef intermediate; Sindhu Right gentler intermediate-friendly)
wave_height_range: Works best 4-6 ft; sweet spot when Nusa Dua/Keramas are pumping; can hold up to 1.5x overhead; minimum size of around 3-4 ft to break properly
bottom_type: Coral reef (shallow live reef ~1 km out; flat lagoon reef shoreward)
best_season: Wet season (November-April; some sources say October-April); peak is January-March when SW/S swells bend through the Bali-Lombok gap and W/SW winds are offshore on the east coast
best_tide: Mid tide is the sweet spot; rising mid-to-high tide preferred; low tide too shallow and fast over the reef; high tide can fatten it out
best_wind: Offshore W to WSW wind (wet season pattern); NW also works for some peaks; dry season SE trades blow onshore here
best_swell_direction: S to SSW (Indian Ocean groundswell bending through the Bali-Lombok gap); SE works for some peaks
swell_consistency_rating: 2-3/5 (sheltered position means it only fires on solid S/SW swells; clean surfable days only ~22% of the time even in January; demands rare alignment of big swell + W wind)
wave_length_meters: approx. 100-250 m for a good Sanur Reef right; Hyatt Reef can run 150-300 m linking the outer to inside section [uncertain]
wave_speed_rating: fast (Sanur Reef main peak is fast and hollow; outer Hyatt steep and hollow, inside softer)
takeoff_zone_difficulty: High at Sanur Reef main peak (steep, powerful drop into shallow reef); moderate at Hyatt Outer; easier at Sindhu Right/inside reform
secondary_peaks: Sanur reef system has 4 named peaks: (1) Sanur Reef/Bomby (main right, advanced), (2) Hyatt Reef (long right, 3 sections - Outer/Main/Inside), (3) Tandjung Sari (Sindhu Right + Sindhu Left), (4) Tandjung Lefts; ~4 km of reef offering options on bigger days
peak_crowd_level: 3-4/5 on firing wet-season days at Sanur Reef main peak (gets busy with local rippers and visiting Aussies); other peaks (Sindhu, Tandjung Lefts) less crowded; 1-2/5 on average days
access: Drive to Sanur (via Sunset Road / Bypass Ngurah Rai); access points: Pantai Mertasari for boat charters to Sanur Reef, Fairmont Sanur Beach Resort beach for main paddle channel, Hyatt Regency Bali beachfront for north peaks, Sindhu beach path for inside reforms; coastal walking path (Sanur boardwalk) links all access points
paddle_out_method: Sanur Reef is over 1 km offshore - the longest paddle in Bali; safer to hire a local boat (jukung) from Pantai Mertasari (negotiate boatman to wait in channel as safety net, approx. IDR 100,000); alternative arm-burning paddle through the boat channel and around the reef from the north; Hyatt Reef accessed by boat from Fairmont channel or long paddle through reef sections near Hyatt Regency
entry_fee: No formal entry fee for the beach; boat charter to Sanur Reef ~IDR 100,000-200,000 one-way / ~IDR 250,000-400,000 return with wait; scooter parking ~IDR 5,000 [uncertain]
recommended_session_time: Dawn patrol (sunrise to ~9 am) when wet-season W winds are lightest offshore and crowd is thinnest; mid-tide rising window often falls early morning in peak season
best_time_of_day: Sunrise to mid-morning before the wind switches and crowd arrives; afternoon often gets onshore unless a strong W wind is locked in
nearby_warung: Massive food scene along Jalan Danau Tamblingan and Sanur Boardwalk - Warung Mak Beng (legendary fish soup), Cafe Batu Jimbar, Sanur Beach Market, Soul on the Beach, plenty of beachfront warungs and cafes from Mertasari to Matahari Terbit
alternative_spot_within_10min: Hyatt Reef, Sindhu Right/Left, Tandjung Lefts (all within the same reef system); slightly further: Serangan reefs (~15-20 min south), Keramas (~30-40 min north on bigger days), Nusa Dua reefs (~25 min south)
boat_charter_required: Recommended for Sanur Reef and Hyatt Reef (1 km+ paddle is exhausting and risky in current); not required for Sindhu Right or inside reforms which are accessible by short paddle from the beach
hazards: Shallow coral reef at low tide (sharp), strong cross-shore current in the channel, long paddle / distance from shore, boat traffic from jukung fishermen and tourist boats, occasional jellyfish in wet season, runoff from Denpasar storm drains after heavy rain
marine_hazards: Live coral reef cuts (most common injury), sea urchins on inside reef, occasional box jellyfish and stingers in wet season, possible stonefish on inside lagoon reef; treat coral cuts with fresh-water flush, vinegar/antiseptic, see clinic if deep [uncertain]
reef_cut_severity_index: High - live shallow coral reef at low tide; reef boots recommended especially for paddle in/out and inside lagoon crossings
rip_current_pattern: Strong cross-current sweeps along the reef system, especially through the boat channel and between peaks; outgoing tide intensifies the pull out to sea; this is also used as the paddle channel [uncertain]
lineup_etiquette: Respect the local lineup hierarchy at the main Sanur Reef peak; local rippers are wave-hungry and possessive when conditions are good; greet locals, do not snake or paddle around, wait your turn, no shouting; smaller peaks (Sindhu, Tandjung) more relaxed
localism_intensity: Medium to high at Sanur Reef main peak (described as 'very wave hungry and possessive' especially on small days); medium at Hyatt; lower at Sindhu Right and outer Hyatt sections
respect_protocol: Acknowledge locals on the paddle out, do not paddle inside on a local's set, share waves on smaller days, do not bring more than 2-3 friends to a peak, support local warungs and jukung boatmen
resident_local_legends: Sanur has produced numerous Indonesian pro surfers; the Hendrawan family and other Sanur-based competitors are local regulars; broader Sanur Surf Club scene [uncertain]
religious_sensitivity: Sanur is a traditional Hindu town - respect ceremonial processions on the beach (Pura Segara temple), Melasti rituals, and offerings (canang sari) on the sand; observe Nyepi (silent day) when no surfing/movement is allowed island-wide; menstruating women traditionally asked to avoid temple grounds
medical_nearby: Sanur has multiple clinics including BIMC Hospital Sanur (international, 24h), Bali Royal Hospital BROS (Sanur), Klinik Pratama Sanur Husada, Prima Medika; for severe trauma RSUP Prof. Ngoerah (Sanglah) Denpasar (~5-7 km)
nearest_clinic_distance_km: BIMC Sanur Hospital approx. 2-3 km from the beach; BROS Sanur approx. 1-2 km; Sanglah Hospital approx. 5-7 km [uncertain]
rescue_jet_ski_available: No regular jet-ski rescue on standby for the Sanur reefs; boatmen/jukung in the channel provide informal safety; lifeguards patrol the shoreline beaches only [uncertain]
emergency_contact: Indonesia general emergency 112; BASARNAS 115; BIMC Sanur Hospital +62 361 3000 911; BROS Sanur +62 361 247 499 [uncertain]
nearest_surf_school: Several schools in Sanur including Surfing Bali Sanur, Bali Surf School Sanur, Aloha Sanur Surf School; lessons taught on the inside reform / Sindhu Right area not on the main Sanur Reef peak
board_rental_availability: Yes - numerous rental stalls along the Sanur beachfront (Pantai Sanur, Mertasari, Matahari Terbit); soft-tops, longboards and shortboards available; approx. IDR 100,000-200,000/day
surf_photographer_available: Limited - some Sanur-based freelance photographers shoot Sanur Reef when it fires; less established than Uluwatu/Keramas; mostly drone shots from accommodation
photographer_recommendation: Search Instagram for Sanur-based water photographers; rates typically IDR 500,000-1,500,000 per session; ask at surf schools for referrals [uncertain]
accommodation_options: Wide range: Fairmont Sanur Beach Bali (luxury, walking distance to main paddle), Hyatt Regency Bali (luxury), Grand Inna Bali Beach (heritage), Maya Sanur Resort, Tandjung Sari Hotel, plenty of mid-range hotels (Mercure, Prime Plaza, Holiday Inn) and budget guesthouses inland of Jalan Danau Tamblingan
drone_allowed: Generally allowed from the beach for personal use; do not fly over resort grounds without permission, respect the long boardwalk and morning sunrise crowd; standard Indonesian drone rules apply [uncertain]
coworking_nearby: Yes - Sanur has a growing digital-nomad scene; coworking options include Genesis Creative Centre, B Work Sanur (limited), Sanur Space, plenty of cafe-coworking (Soul on the Beach, Bali Buda) [uncertain]
avg_lesson_price: Group surf lesson approx. IDR 400,000-600,000 (USD 25-40) at Sanur surf schools; private lesson IDR 700,000-1,200,000; tends to be cheaper than Canggu/Uluwatu [uncertain]
board_rental_daily: Approx. IDR 100,000-200,000 (USD 7-15) per day for shortboard/longboard at Sanur beachfront rentals; softboards from IDR 75,000 [uncertain]
water_quality_rating: Generally fair to good in dry season (sheltered lagoon stays clearer than Kuta/Seminyak); drops in wet season due to Denpasar storm-drain runoff and rivermouth output; ongoing reef restoration projects on the inner reef [uncertain]
annual_competition_window: Sanur Reef occasionally hosts national-level Indonesian Surfing Championship Tour (ISC) events and longboard contests in the wet season window (Dec-Mar); not a regular WSL CT venue [uncertain]
community_events: Sanur Village Festival annually (cultural, includes water sports demos); regular dawn-patrol crew on the boardwalk; full-moon yoga sessions on the beach; Sanur Surf Club community paddle-outs [uncertain]
